blocks.text: Clap your hands, all you peoples; shout to God with loud songs of joy.

Ps 47: Acclaim the Lord the king of heaven and earth! An enthronement hymn, like Pss 93; 95–99, celebrating the reigning over heaven and earth. Some scholars have suggested that it was written for a New Year festival celebrating the ’s kingship.
For the Lord, the Most High, is awesome, a great king over all the earth.
2 : , better “the Great King,” a title of suzerains in Mesopotamia, Egypt, and the Hittite kingdom.
He subdued peoples under us, and nations under our feet.
He chose our heritage for us, the pride of Jacob whom he loves. Selah

1–4 : Presupposed is the ’s universal victory that is also celebrated in Pss 2; 46; 48; 76; 93. Israel’s task is to offer a suitable welcome and receive the territory ( , v. 4) that the victorious assigns.
God has gone up with a shout, the Lord with the sound of a trumpet.
5 : , ascended the throne as in 2 Sam 6.15; Isa 14.13. The and were part of the ritual proclaiming the new king (see 1 Kings 1.39–49).
Sing praises to God, sing praises; sing praises to our King, sing praises.
For God is the king of all the earth; sing praises with a psalm.
God is king over the nations; God sits on his holy throne.
8 : , perhaps better translated as actions in a ceremony: “has become king … has taken his seat on the throne.”
The princes of the peoples gather as the people of the God of Abraham. For the shields of the earth belong to God; he is highly exalted.
9 : , an emendation yields “The princes of the peoples gather the people of the God of Abraham.” , i.e., rulers (by metonymy).
